findings.title: Integer Division Rounding Errors in Share/Asset Conversions
findings.description: All share-to-asset and asset-to-share conversions in AbstractYieldStrategy use integer division that rounds down. VIRTUAL_SHARES/VIRTUAL_YIELD_TOKENS mitigation (1e6) reduces but does not eliminate rounding errors. Repeated conversions compound precision loss. Attackers can exploit rounding dust through repeated deposit/withdraw cycles.
findings.recommendation: Consider using a higher precision virtual buffer or implementing a rounding direction policy that favors the protocol.

findings.title: Oracle Price Inversion Precision Loss
findings.description: The price inversion formula d^2/x used in PendlePTOracle and Curve2TokenOracle loses precision for certain values. When baseToUSD is very large or very small relative to baseToUSDDecimals, the division truncates significant digits.
findings.recommendation: Use higher precision intermediate values for oracle price inversion calculations.

findings.title: Multi-Step Precision Loss in LP Token Valuation
findings.description: Multiple sequential divisions in AbstractLPOracle LP token valuation compound rounding errors. Each step truncates, and errors accumulate through the calculation chain. The final LP token value can deviate from true value, potentially causing incorrect collateral valuations.
findings.recommendation: Aggregate operations to reduce the number of intermediate divisions, or use higher precision intermediate types.

findings.title: Withdraw Request Tokenization Rounding Errors
findings.description: Withdraw request tokenization in AbstractWithdrawRequestManager uses integer division that rounds down. When partial withdrawals or tokenizations occur, rounding errors accumulate, leaving small amounts of tokens stranded in the contract.
findings.recommendation: Track and account for rounding dust in withdraw request tokenization to prevent stranded tokens.

findings.title: Reward Accumulation Rounding Dust
findings.description: Reward calculations in AbstractRewardManager use integer division that rounds down. The 'reward debt' pattern minimizes but doesn't eliminate dust. Small amounts of rewards accumulate in the contract over time.
findings.recommendation: Implement periodic dust sweep mechanism or accept dust accumulation as a known limitation.

findings.title: Lack of Access Control on finalizeRequestManual() Allows Griefing
findings.description: finalizeRequestManual(address vault, address account) has NO access control modifier. An attacker can call this before the vault's finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest(), causing the vault's call to _finalizeWithdraw() to hit the early return on the finalized flag. While mathematically equivalent, this enables griefing and could interfere with expected atomic workflows.
findings.recommendation: Add access control to finalizeRequestManual() to restrict it to authorized callers (e.g., only vault contracts or onlyOwner).

findings.title: forceWithdraw() Missing Authorization Check Enables Griefing of Unhealthy Positions
findings.description: forceWithdraw(address account, address vault, bytes calldata data) has NO isAuthorization modifier. While intentionally designed to allow liquidators to force withdraw initiation on underwater positions, any address can trigger this, enabling griefing or front-running of legitimate liquidations.
findings.recommendation: Consider adding a minimal access control or incentive mechanism to prevent griefing of unhealthy positions.

findings.title: initialize() in Initializable Vulnerable to Front-Running on Proxy Deployment
findings.description: If a proxy is deployed without initialization data and someone calls initialize() before the intended initializer, they can set arbitrary initialization parameters. All _initialize() implementations decode their data parameter for admin addresses and vault configurations.
findings.recommendation: Always deploy proxies with initialization data in the constructor, or use an initializer modifier pattern that locks to the deployer.

findings.title: Trade Deadline Set to block.timestamp Enables MEV/Sandwich Attacks
findings.description: All trade constructions across the protocol set deadline: block.timestamp, meaning trades can be included in the current block and are vulnerable to sandwich attacks. Found in AbstractSingleSidedLP, AbstractStakingStrategy, PendlePT, PendlePT_sUSDe, and AbstractWithdrawRequestManager.
findings.recommendation: Use a future block timestamp or block.number + N for trade deadlines to prevent same-block MEV extraction.

findings.title: Curve2TokenOracle Spot Price Manipulation via Pool Imbalance
findings.description: The oracle calculates LP token value using get_dy from Curve pools for spot price comparison with a fixed dyAmount. A large trade can temporarily shift the Curve pool balance, changing the get_dy result. If manipulated spot price stays within deviation bounds, the manipulation succeeds, affecting collateral valuation.
findings.recommendation: Use time-weighted average prices or increase deviation bounds check sensitivity. Consider using Curve's get_virtual_price() instead of get_dy for spot pricing.

findings.title: Fee Accrual Race Condition with Same-Block Deposit/Withdraw
findings.description: Fee accrual only updates if s_lastFeeAccrualTime != block.timestamp. An attacker could time deposits/withdrawals within the same block to avoid fee accrual windows, minimizing their fee exposure.
findings.recommendation: Consider tracking per-block fee accrual or accepting same-block fee avoidance as a known limitation with negligible impact.

findings.title: Dinero Withdraw Finalization Logic Inverted
findings.description: canFinalizeWithdrawRequest in Dinero.sol returns true when PirexETH.outstandingRedemptions() > totalAssets. This logic is inverted - it should return true when assets are sufficient (totalAssets >= outstandingRedemptions), not when there is a deficit. Withdraws may be blocked when assets are sufficient, or allowed when assets are insufficient.
findings.recommendation: Invert the comparison to: return totalAssets >= PirexETH.outstandingRedemptions().

findings.title: Effective Supply Underflow Risk When Escrowed Shares Exceed Total Supply
findings.description: effectiveSupply() returns totalSupply() - s_escrowedShares + VIRTUAL_SHARES. If s_escrowedShares exceeds totalSupply() by more than VIRTUAL_SHARES (1e6), the function reverts, freezing all vault operations that depend on effectiveSupply().
findings.recommendation: Add a safe check to prevent s_escrowedShares from exceeding totalSupply(), or use a SafeMath pattern that handles the edge case.

findings.title: Oracle Zero Price Division by Zero Risk in Share Conversions
findings.description: convertToShares calls convertYieldTokenToAsset() which retrieves an oracle price. If the oracle returns zero, the division causes a revert, halting all share/asset conversions and potentially locking user funds.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit zero-check on oracle prices before division, with a descriptive error message.

findings.title: Chainlink Oracle Stale Data Not Checked in Curve2TokenOracle and PendlePTOracle
findings.description: Both oracles call baseToUSDOracle.latestRoundData() but only check require(baseToUSD > 0). No validation of updatedAt freshness, roundId, or answeredInRound. A stale or frozen Chainlink price could be used silently, leading to incorrect collateral valuations.
findings.recommendation: Add freshness checks: require(block.timestamp - updatedAt <= MAX_STALENESS) and require(answeredInRound >= roundId).

findings.title: No Slippage Guard on sUSDe to sDAI Trade Leg in PendlePT_sUSDe
findings.description: The first trade leg (sUSDe -> sDAI) explicitly sets limit: 0 with comment 'no slippage guard is set here, it is enforced in the second leg'. If the first leg suffers slippage, the user receives fewer sDAI. The second leg slippage check only protects DAI -> asset conversion, not the initial swap.
findings.recommendation: Add slippage protection to the first trade leg, or calculate the expected sDAI output and verify against a minimum.

findings.title: Silent Reward Token Transfer Failures in AbstractRewardManager
findings.description: _claimRewardToken uses try/catch around IEIP20NonStandard(rewardToken).transfer() and silently emits VaultRewardTransfer with 0 on failure. Users accumulate reward debt but never receive tokens if the reward token reverts (blacklist, insufficient balance). The reward debt is still updated, permanently losing those rewards.
findings.recommendation: Only update reward debt when the transfer succeeds, or provide a mechanism to retry failed reward transfers.

findings.title: Unlimited Token Approvals to External Protocols
findings.description: Multiple contracts grant type(uint256).max approvals to external protocols (Convex booster, Curve pool/gauge, lending routers). If any of these external contracts are compromised, all approved tokens could be drained.
findings.recommendation: Use per-transaction approval amounts or implement approval limits based on expected usage.

findings.title: DelegateCall to Dynamic TradingModule Implementation
findings.description: _executeTrade fetches the TradingModule implementation address via nProxy.getImplementation() and delegatecalls to it. If the TradingModule proxy is upgraded maliciously, arbitrary code executes in the vault's context with full storage access.
findings.recommendation: Cache the implementation address with a timelock on changes, or use a call pattern instead of delegatecall where possible.

findings.title: Ethena Clone Address Used as Request ID Breaks on Implementation Change
findings.description: The requestId for Ethena withdraws is uint256(uint160(address(holder))) where holder is a cloned contract. If HOLDER_IMPLEMENTATION is ever changed via upgrade, existing requestIds would point to non-existent contracts, making existing withdraw requests unresolvable.
findings.recommendation: Use a sequential nonce-based requestId instead of the contract address, or ensure the HOLDER_IMPLEMENTATION is immutable.

findings.title: PendlePTOracle SY vs Asset Rate Source Divergence During Depegging
findings.description: The oracle uses either SY oracle rate or Asset oracle rate based on immutable useSyOracleRate. During depegging events between the SY token and its underlying asset, the oracle price could diverge from the true asset value, enabling arbitrage at the protocol's expense.
findings.recommendation: Consider using the asset oracle rate as a cross-check against the SY rate, with a deviation threshold.

raw_output:
# 🔐 Security Review — Notional Finance

---

## Scope

| | |
| --- | --- |
| **Mode** | ALL (31 files) |
| **Files reviewed** | All .sol in src/ excluding interfaces/, lib/, mocks/, test/ |
| **Agents** | vector-scan, math-precision, access-control, economic-security, invariant, periphery, first-principles |

---

## Findings

[65] **F-001. Integer Division Rounding Errors in Share/Asset Conversions**

Agents: math-precision, invariant · Confidence: high

**Description**
All share-to-asset and asset-to-share conversions in AbstractYieldStrategy use integer division that rounds down. VIRTUAL_SHARES/VIRTUAL_YIELD_TOKENS mitigation (1e6) reduces but does not eliminate rounding errors. Repeated conversions compound precision loss. Attackers can exploit rounding dust through repeated deposit/withdraw cycles.

**Location:** `src/AbstractYieldStrategy.sol` lines 100-113, 411

**Fix**
Consider using a higher precision virtual buffer or implementing a rounding direction policy that favors the protocol.

---

[65] **F-002. Oracle Price Inversion Precision Loss**

Agents: math-precision · Confidence: medium

**Description**
The price inversion formula d^2/x used in PendlePTOracle and Curve2TokenOracle loses precision for certain values. When baseToUSD is very large or very small relative to baseToUSDDecimals, the division truncates significant digits.

**Location:** `src/oracles/PendlePTOracle.sol` lines 85

**Fix**
Use higher precision intermediate values for oracle price inversion calculations.

---

[65] **F-003. Multi-Step Precision Loss in LP Token Valuation**

Agents: math-precision · Confidence: medium

**Description**
Multiple sequential divisions in AbstractLPOracle LP token valuation compound rounding errors. Each step truncates, and errors accumulate through the calculation chain. The final LP token value can deviate from true value, potentially causing incorrect collateral valuations.

**Location:** `src/oracles/AbstractLPOracle.sol` lines 85, 93-94, 103-104, 109

**Fix**
Aggregate operations to reduce the number of intermediate divisions, or use higher precision intermediate types.

---

[65] **F-004. Withdraw Request Tokenization Rounding Errors**

Agents: math-precision · Confidence: medium

**Description**
Withdraw request tokenization in AbstractWithdrawRequestManager uses integer division that rounds down. When partial withdrawals or tokenizations occur, rounding errors accumulate, leaving small amounts of tokens stranded in the contract.

**Location:** `src/withdraws/AbstractWithdrawRequestManager.sol` lines 136, 195, 225, 240, 326

**Fix**
Track and account for rounding dust in withdraw request tokenization to prevent stranded tokens.

---

[40] **F-005. Reward Accumulation Rounding Dust**

Agents: math-precision · Confidence: medium

**Description**
Reward calculations in AbstractRewardManager use integer division that rounds down. The 'reward debt' pattern minimizes but doesn't eliminate dust. Small amounts of rewards accumulate in the contract over time.

**Location:** `src/rewards/AbstractRewardManager.sol` lines 231, 268, 309-310

**Fix**
Implement periodic dust sweep mechanism or accept dust accumulation as a known limitation.

---

[65] **F-006. Lack of Access Control on finalizeRequestManual() Allows Griefing**

Agents: access-control · Confidence: high

**Description**
finalizeRequestManual(address vault, address account) has NO access control modifier. An attacker can call this before the vault's finalizeAndRedeemWithdrawRequest(), causing the vault's call to _finalizeWithdraw() to hit the early return on the finalized flag. While mathematically equivalent, this enables griefing and could interfere with expected atomic workflows.

**Location:** `src/withdraws/AbstractWithdrawRequestManager.sol` lines 149-159

**Fix**
Add access control to finalizeRequestManual() to restrict it to authorized callers (e.g., only vault contracts or onlyOwner).

---

[65] **F-007. forceWithdraw() Missing Authorization Check Enables Griefing of Unhealthy Positions**

Agents: access-control · Confidence: high

**Description**
forceWithdraw(address account, address vault, bytes calldata data) has NO isAuthorization modifier. While intentionally designed to allow liquidators to force withdraw initiation on underwater positions, any address can trigger this, enabling griefing or front-running of legitimate liquidations.

**Location:** `src/routers/AbstractLendingRouter.sol` lines 182-192

**Fix**
Consider adding a minimal access control or incentive mechanism to prevent griefing of unhealthy positions.

---

[85] **F-008. initialize() in Initializable Vulnerable to Front-Running on Proxy Deployment**

Agents: access-control · Confidence: medium

**Description**
If a proxy is deployed without initialization data and someone calls initialize() before the intended initializer, they can set arbitrary initialization parameters. All _initialize() implementations decode their data parameter for admin addresses and vault configurations.

**Location:** `src/proxy/Initializable.sol` lines 13-17

**Fix**
Always deploy proxies with initialization data in the constructor, or use an initializer modifier pattern that locks to the deployer.

---

[65] **F-009. Trade Deadline Set to block.timestamp Enables MEV/Sandwich Attacks**

Agents: economic-security · Confidence: high

**Description**
All trade constructions across the protocol set deadline: block.timestamp, meaning trades can be included in the current block and are vulnerable to sandwich attacks. Found in AbstractSingleSidedLP, AbstractStakingStrategy, PendlePT, PendlePT_sUSDe, and AbstractWithdrawRequestManager.

**Location:** `src/single-sided-lp/AbstractSingleSidedLP.sol` lines 201, 243

**Fix**
Use a future block timestamp or block.number + N for trade deadlines to prevent same-block MEV extraction.

---

[65] **F-010. Curve2TokenOracle Spot Price Manipulation via Pool Imbalance**

Agents: economic-security, first-principles · Confidence: medium

**Description**
The oracle calculates LP token value using get_dy from Curve pools for spot price comparison with a fixed dyAmount. A large trade can temporarily shift the Curve pool balance, changing the get_dy result. If manipulated spot price stays within deviation bounds, the manipulation succeeds, affecting collateral valuation.

**Location:** `src/oracles/Curve2TokenOracle.sol` lines 55-83

**Fix**
Use time-weighted average prices or increase deviation bounds check sensitivity. Consider using Curve's get_virtual_price() instead of get_dy for spot pricing.

---

[40] **F-011. Fee Accrual Race Condition with Same-Block Deposit/Withdraw**

Agents: economic-security, first-principles · Confidence: medium

**Description**
Fee accrual only updates if s_lastFeeAccrualTime != block.timestamp. An attacker could time deposits/withdrawals within the same block to avoid fee accrual windows, minimizing their fee exposure.

**Location:** `src/AbstractYieldStrategy.sol` lines 326-331

**Fix**
Consider tracking per-block fee accrual or accepting same-block fee avoidance as a known limitation with negligible impact.

---

[85] **F-012. Dinero Withdraw Finalization Logic Inverted**

Agents: invariant, periphery · Confidence: high

**Description**
canFinalizeWithdrawRequest in Dinero.sol returns true when PirexETH.outstandingRedemptions() > totalAssets. This logic is inverted - it should return true when assets are sufficient (totalAssets >= outstandingRedemptions), not when there is a deficit. Withdraws may be blocked when assets are sufficient, or allowed when assets are insufficient.

**Location:** `src/withdraws/Dinero.sol` lines 71-88

**Fix**
Invert the comparison to: return totalAssets >= PirexETH.outstandingRedemptions().

---

[65] **F-013. Effective Supply Underflow Risk When Escrowed Shares Exceed Total Supply**

Agents: invariant · Confidence: medium

**Description**
effectiveSupply() returns totalSupply() - s_escrowedShares + VIRTUAL_SHARES. If s_escrowedShares exceeds totalSupply() by more than VIRTUAL_SHARES (1e6), the function reverts, freezing all vault operations that depend on effectiveSupply().

**Location:** `src/AbstractYieldStrategy.sol` lines 149-151

**Fix**
Add a safe check to prevent s_escrowedShares from exceeding totalSupply(), or use a SafeMath pattern that handles the edge case.

---

[65] **F-014. Oracle Zero Price Division by Zero Risk in Share Conversions**

Agents: invariant · Confidence: medium

**Description**
convertToShares calls convertYieldTokenToAsset() which retrieves an oracle price. If the oracle returns zero, the division causes a revert, halting all share/asset conversions and potentially locking user funds.

**Location:** `src/AbstractYieldStrategy.sol` lines 110-115

**Fix**
Add an explicit zero-check on oracle prices before division, with a descriptive error message.

---

[85] **F-015. Chainlink Oracle Stale Data Not Checked in Curve2TokenOracle and PendlePTOracle**

Agents: periphery, economic-security, first-principles · Confidence: high

**Description**
Both oracles call baseToUSDOracle.latestRoundData() but only check require(baseToUSD > 0). No validation of updatedAt freshness, roundId, or answeredInRound. A stale or frozen Chainlink price could be used silently, leading to incorrect collateral valuations.

**Location:** `src/oracles/Curve2TokenOracle.sol` lines 96-101

**Fix**
Add freshness checks: require(block.timestamp - updatedAt <= MAX_STALENESS) and require(answeredInRound >= roundId).

---

[85] **F-016. No Slippage Guard on sUSDe to sDAI Trade Leg in PendlePT_sUSDe**

Agents: periphery · Confidence: medium

**Description**
The first trade leg (sUSDe -> sDAI) explicitly sets limit: 0 with comment 'no slippage guard is set here, it is enforced in the second leg'. If the first leg suffers slippage, the user receives fewer sDAI. The second leg slippage check only protects DAI -> asset conversion, not the initial swap.

**Location:** `src/staking/PendlePT_sUSDe.sol` lines 38-39

**Fix**
Add slippage protection to the first trade leg, or calculate the expected sDAI output and verify against a minimum.

---

[65] **F-017. Silent Reward Token Transfer Failures in AbstractRewardManager**

Agents: periphery · Confidence: high

**Description**
_claimRewardToken uses try/catch around IEIP20NonStandard(rewardToken).transfer() and silently emits VaultRewardTransfer with 0 on failure. Users accumulate reward debt but never receive tokens if the reward token reverts (blacklist, insufficient balance). The reward debt is still updated, permanently losing those rewards.

**Location:** `src/rewards/AbstractRewardManager.sol` lines 241-253

**Fix**
Only update reward debt when the transfer succeeds, or provide a mechanism to retry failed reward transfers.

---

[65] **F-018. Unlimited Token Approvals to External Protocols**

Agents: periphery · Confidence: medium

**Description**
Multiple contracts grant type(uint256).max approvals to external protocols (Convex booster, Curve pool/gauge, lending routers). If any of these external contracts are compromised, all approved tokens could be drained.

**Location:** `src/rewards/ConvexRewardManager.sol` lines 25

**Fix**
Use per-transaction approval amounts or implement approval limits based on expected usage.

---

[65] **F-019. DelegateCall to Dynamic TradingModule Implementation**

Agents: periphery, access-control, first-principles · Confidence: medium

**Description**
_executeTrade fetches the TradingModule implementation address via nProxy.getImplementation() and delegatecalls to it. If the TradingModule proxy is upgraded maliciously, arbitrary code executes in the vault's context with full storage access.

**Location:** `src/AbstractYieldStrategy.sol` lines 369-374

**Fix**
Cache the implementation address with a timelock on changes, or use a call pattern instead of delegatecall where possible.

---

[40] **F-020. Ethena Clone Address Used as Request ID Breaks on Implementation Change**

Agents: first-principles · Confidence: medium

**Description**
The requestId for Ethena withdraws is uint256(uint160(address(holder))) where holder is a cloned contract. If HOLDER_IMPLEMENTATION is ever changed via upgrade, existing requestIds would point to non-existent contracts, making existing withdraw requests unresolvable.

**Location:** `src/withdraws/Ethena.sol` lines 76-80

**Fix**
Use a sequential nonce-based requestId instead of the contract address, or ensure the HOLDER_IMPLEMENTATION is immutable.

---

[40] **F-021. PendlePTOracle SY vs Asset Rate Source Divergence During Depegging**

Agents: economic-security, first-principles · Confidence: low

**Description**
The oracle uses either SY oracle rate or Asset oracle rate based on immutable useSyOracleRate. During depegging events between the SY token and its underlying asset, the oracle price could diverge from the true asset value, enabling arbitrage at the protocol's expense.

**Location:** `src/oracles/PendlePTOracle.sol` lines 61-65

**Fix**
Consider using the asset oracle rate as a cross-check against the SY rate, with a deviation threshold.

---


## Findings List

| --- | --- | --- |
| F-001 | medium | Integer Division Rounding Errors in Share/Asset Conversions |
| F-002 | medium | Oracle Price Inversion Precision Loss |
| F-003 | medium | Multi-Step Precision Loss in LP Token Valuation |
| F-004 | medium | Withdraw Request Tokenization Rounding Errors |
| F-005 | low | Reward Accumulation Rounding Dust |
| F-006 | medium | Lack of Access Control on finalizeRequestManual() Allows Griefing |
| F-007 | medium | forceWithdraw() Missing Authorization Check Enables Griefing of Unhealthy Positions |
| F-008 | high | initialize() in Initializable Vulnerable to Front-Running on Proxy Deployment |
| F-009 | medium | Trade Deadline Set to block.timestamp Enables MEV/Sandwich Attacks |
| F-010 | medium | Curve2TokenOracle Spot Price Manipulation via Pool Imbalance |
| F-011 | low | Fee Accrual Race Condition with Same-Block Deposit/Withdraw |
| F-012 | high | Dinero Withdraw Finalization Logic Inverted |
| F-013 | medium | Effective Supply Underflow Risk When Escrowed Shares Exceed Total Supply |
| F-014 | medium | Oracle Zero Price Division by Zero Risk in Share Conversions |
| F-015 | high | Chainlink Oracle Stale Data Not Checked in Curve2TokenOracle and PendlePTOracle |
| F-016 | high | No Slippage Guard on sUSDe to sDAI Trade Leg in PendlePT_sUSDe |
| F-017 | medium | Silent Reward Token Transfer Failures in AbstractRewardManager |
| F-018 | medium | Unlimited Token Approvals to External Protocols |
| F-019 | medium | DelegateCall to Dynamic TradingModule Implementation |
| F-020 | low | Ethena Clone Address Used as Request ID Breaks on Implementation Change |
| F-021 | low | PendlePTOracle SY vs Asset Rate Source Divergence During Depegging |

---

> ⚠️ This review was performed by AI assistants using the pashov/skills solidity-auditor framework. AI analysis can never verify the complete absence of vulnerabilities and no guarantee of security is given.
